common_name: Caribbean Stylo (Carib Stylo, Hamata Stylosanthes, Pencil Flower)

planting.note: Direct sow in full sun in lean, well-drained, sandy to gravelly soil; Stylosanthes hamata is one of the most drought-tolerant, heat-adapted legume groundcovers for zone 9-12, widely used throughout the Caribbean and tropical Americas for range improvement, erosion control, and nitrogen fixation in poor, dry, sandy soils where most other groundcovers fail to establish. Very low growing (6–18 inches), it spreads laterally and self-seeds freely. The small yellow-orange pea flowers are produced almost continuously through the warm months and attract small native bees. In zone 9, the plant may be killed by hard frosts but often reseeds itself the following spring in appropriate conditions.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in any lean, well-drained, sandy, or gravelly soil — Caribbean Stylo establishes readily in poor, dry conditions and is one of the most useful, low-maintenance leguminous groundcovers available for zone 9-12 landscapes with challenging, infertile, dry soils. Stylosanthes hamata is a low-growing, semi-erect, annual to perennial herbaceous legume in the Fabaceae family (tribe Aeschynomeneae) native to the Caribbean, Mexico, Central and South America — widely distributed through the tropical Americas in open, sunny, dry, disturbed grassland and savanna habitats. The plant produces slender, branching stems bearing small, trifoliate leaves with narrow leaflets and, through the warm months, tiny (0.25-inch) yellow to orange-yellow flowers in small clusters. As a legume, Stylosanthes hamata fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodule symbiosis, steadily improving the fertility of poor, sandy soils over time. Widely used in tropical range management and pasture improvement throughout the Caribbean, Australia, and tropical Africa for its ability to establish in degraded, dry, sandy range land where more demanding forages will not survive. In zone 9-12 ornamental gardens, Caribbean Stylo serves as a drought-tolerant, nitrogen-fixing groundcover for difficult, sunny, dry areas — roadsides, slopes, and sandy fills — and produces a season-long display of small yellow flowers attractive to native bees and small pollinators. Self-seeds freely to maintain coverage. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Stylosanthes hamata for Florida range and cover crop use
USDA NRCS Plant Materials Center — Stylosanthes hamata: Caribbean Stylo for Gulf Coast use
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Legumes for Gulf Coast Cover Cropping and Range Management
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Cover crops and range legumes for zone 9 Gulf Coast

common_name: Betel Pepper (Betel Vine, Paan Leaf, Sirih)

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil with support for climbing; Piper betle is a shade-loving, moisture-preferring vine that grows naturally in the understory of tropical forests and performs best in humid, partially shaded conditions with regular watering. The glossy, heart-shaped leaves are harvested for traditional use throughout South and Southeast Asia — fresh betel leaves are used as a digestive herb, as a wrapper for the traditional 'paan' preparation, and in various medicinal applications across Indian, Sri Lankan, Indonesian, Malaysian, Thai, and Vietnamese traditions. In zone 9, protect from frost; in zone 10-12, grows as a perennial climbing vine. Non-toxic in moderate culinary/herbal amounts.

directions: Grow in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil with a support for climbing — Betel Pepper grows most luxuriantly in the warm, moist, shaded understory conditions similar to its native habitat and will languish in full sun or dry soil. Piper betle is a perennial, climbing vine in the Piperaceae family native to South and Southeast Asia — the same genus as black pepper (P. nigrum) — and one of the most culturally and economically significant plants in the traditional medicine and daily life of South and Southeast Asia. The plant produces heart-shaped, glossy, smooth, dark-green leaves on a climbing, node-rooting vine that ascends trees and structures by adhesive root pads. The leaves are the harvest product: fresh betel leaves (called 'paan' in South Asian tradition) are harvested and used in the preparation of the traditional South Asian chewing combination of areca nut (from Areca catechu), slaked lime, and betel leaf — a preparation with mild stimulant effects consumed by hundreds of millions of people across India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, and Southeast Asia. Beyond this traditional use, fresh betel leaves are used as a cooking wrapper, an herbal digestive, and in various ethnobotanical and Ayurvedic applications throughout the Asian traditional medicine systems. The leaves contain chavicol, eugenol, and other phenolic compounds that contribute their distinctive flavor and mild bioactivity. Grown in zone 10-12 as a permanent perennial shade vine, or as an annual-treated container plant in zone 9. Non-toxic in culinary amounts.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Piper betle for Florida herb gardens
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Piper betle: betel vine botany and cultivation
University of Hawaii CTAHR — Piper betle: betel pepper for Pacific and tropical gardens
Purdue University NewCROP — Piper betle: the betel leaf species profile

common_name: Wave of Love Anthurium (Plowman's Anthurium, Ruffle Anthurium, Bird's Nest Anthurium)

planting.note: Grow in shade to partial shade in well-drained, moist, humus-rich, aerated aroid mix; Anthurium plowmanii is a bird's-nest type anthurium that produces an upright rosette of large, strongly ruffled and undulate leaves 24–36 inches long with dramatically wavy, crinkled margins — the 'wave' in the popular name 'Wave of Love.' Unlike the climbing velvet anthuriums, it is a terrestrial, self-heading plant that spreads by producing offsets from the base. It grows best in warm, humid, deeply shaded conditions. Water moderately, allowing the mix to partially dry between waterings. CAUTION: calcium oxalate in all parts; toxic to cats and dogs.

directions: Grow in shade to partial shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, chunky mix — Wave of Love Anthurium grows most beautifully in the warm, humid, deeply shaded conditions of a sheltered tropical garden or protected patio in zone 10-12, where its unusually textured, undulate leaves create a dramatic, bold-textured presence. Anthurium plowmanii is a large, terrestrial, bird's-nest type anthurium in the Araceae family native to Bolivia, Peru, and Brazil, belonging to the section Pachyneurium — the group of anthuriums that form upright, self-heading rosettes rather than climbing vines. The plant produces an erect, open rosette of large (24–36 inch) leaves that are narrowly elliptic to strap-like, with the most distinctive feature being their strongly undulate, ruffled, crinkled margins — the leaf edges are so dramatically wavy and ruffled that they form a complex, three-dimensional edge pattern unlike any other commonly grown anthurium. The leaf surface is dark to medium green, smooth, and glossy, with the contrasting undulate margin creating a rippled effect. The plant produces inflorescences with a narrow spathe and erect, pencil-like spadix, followed by small berry clusters. Over time, the central rosette produces side offsets that can be separated for propagation. A bold, architectural shade plant of genuine presence for zone 10-12 sheltered outdoor spaces. CAUTION: toxic to pets if ingested.
sources: International Aroid Society — Anthurium plowmanii: Wave of Love cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium plowmanii species profile
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Bird's Nest Anthuriums for Florida shade gardens
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to cats and dogs)

common_name: Radicans Anthurium (Crawling Anthurium, Trailing Anthurium, Rugose-Leaved Anthurium)

planting.note: Grow in shade to partial shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, chunky aroid mix; Anthurium radicans is a small, creeping, trailing anthurium from Brazil that produces heavily corrugated, bumpy, dark forest-green leaves on trailing to pendant stems — the bumpy, quilted leaf texture is the primary ornamental feature, giving each leaf a three-dimensional, sculptural quality reminiscent of hammered metal or fabric brocade. Excellent in hanging baskets or as a groundcover in shaded tropical gardens. Produces small berry clusters on short spikes. Best in warm, humid, shaded conditions. CAUTION: calcium oxalate in all parts; toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in shade to partial shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich mix — Radicans Anthurium grows most compactly and textural in warm, humid, deeply shaded conditions and is an excellent choice for hanging baskets, low-light terrariums, or as a shaded ground-cover in zone 10-12. Anthurium radicans is a small, creeping, stoloniferous anthurium in the Araceae family native to southeastern Brazil, belonging to the section Cardiolonchium in Anthurium (the same broad group as A. crystallinum and A. clarinervium) but with a very different, low-growing, creeping habit rather than the large-leaved, upright form of those species. The plant produces stems that trail and creep outward, rooting at nodes where they contact moist substrate, producing a spreading mat of closely-set leaves in shaded locations. Each individual leaf is small to medium (4–8 inches), broadly heart-shaped to ovate, dark forest-green, and densely covered with small, raised bumps and irregular surface corrugations that create a strongly rugose (wrinkled, bumpy) texture — as if each leaf were molded from hammered metal or brocade fabric. This texture is constant throughout the leaf surface and gives the plant a tactile quality unusual in aroids. The creeping, rooting stems make A. radicans naturally well-suited to hanging baskets (where it trails over the edges) or to spreading as a groundcover in shaded tropical planters. It is also commonly grown hybridized with A. dressleri (producing A. ×dressleri hybrids) to enhance the leaf texture. CAUTION: toxic to pets if ingested.
sources: International Aroid Society — Anthurium radicans cultivation notes
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Anthurium radicans species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Small climbing and creeping aroids for Florida shade gardens
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Anthurium (toxic to cats and dogs)

common_name: Long-Lobed Alocasia (Malaysian Monster, Longiloba Elephant Ear, Tiger Taro)

planting.note: Plant rhizome or division in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in a warm, humid location; Alocasia longiloba is a striking medium-to-large elephant ear from Malaysia and Borneo with dark, blue-green, slightly metallic leaf blades marked with pale blue-gray to silver veins and very long basal lobes that extend downward, giving each leaf an elongated, blade-like silhouette quite different from the broader-lobed appearance of most elephant ears. Grows best in high humidity, warm temperatures (above 60°F), and consistent moisture. May die back in zone 9b freezes but resprouts from rhizomes in spring. CAUTION: calcium oxalate in all parts; toxic to cats, dogs, and humans if consumed raw.

directions: Plant in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Long-Lobed Alocasia grows best in the warm, humid, partially shaded conditions of a protected tropical garden or shaded patio and produces its most dramatic foliage in consistent warmth and moisture. Alocasia longiloba is a rhizomatous, medium-to-large perennial aroid in the Araceae family native to Peninsular Malaysia, Borneo, and Sumatra — one of the many ornamentally valuable Alocasia species from the tropical forests of Southeast Asia. The plant produces large, elongated, leaf blades on sturdy petioles: each leaf is narrowly sagittate (arrowhead-shaped) with characteristically long, drooping basal lobes that extend significantly downward, creating a leaf silhouette that is more blade-like and elongated than the broader shield-shape of Alocasia macrorrhizos or A. odora. The upper leaf surface is dark blue-green with a slightly metallic or satiny sheen, and the prominent midrib and primary veins are pale blue-gray to silvery-white — creating a strong, graphic contrast of dark ground and light veins. The underside of the leaf is pale gray-green to lavender. The overall appearance is of a dramatically architectural, dark-and-silver tropical foliage plant of considerable beauty. Multiple horticultural forms and cultivars exist under the name longiloba with varying degrees of vein prominence and leaf color intensity. In zone 9, plants die to the ground in hard freezes but regrow vigorously from rhizomes in spring. CAUTION: all Alocasia parts are toxic if consumed raw — calcium oxalate crystals cause severe irritation.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Alocasia longiloba species profile
International Aroid Society — Alocasia longiloba cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Elephant ears (Alocasia and Colocasia) for Florida landscapes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Alocasia (toxic to cats and dogs)
